---
title: "Match the Git command to the GUI action"
subtitle: "A live matching activity for the Git for Science workshop"
format:
  typst:
    papersize: a4
    margin:
      x: 2cm
      y: 2cm
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "Arial"
---

## How this works

In this workshop we drive Git through a graphical interface: buttons in the
RStudio Git pane and on GitHub. Every click runs a Git command underneath.
Clicking the push button *is* `git push`. Using the interface is not
"cheating"; it runs the same commands a terminal user types, and it is the
bridge to the agentic-coding follow-up where an agent drives Git by naming
those same commands.

**The activity (10 minutes, in pairs):** draw a line from each Git command on
the left to its matching GUI action on the right, then we compare as a group.
One item on the left is a GitHub-side action, not a Git command; matching it is
part of the point.

## Learner sheet

The dots mark where to start and end each line.

```{=typst}
// A boxed item with a connection dot. `side` is left or right: the dot sits
// on the inner edge facing the gap between the two columns.
#let item(body, side: right) = {
  let dot = circle(radius: 2pt, fill: black)
  block(
    width: 100%,
    inset: 5pt,
    stroke: 0.5pt,
    radius: 3pt,
    if side == right {
      grid(columns: (1fr, auto), column-gutter: 6pt, align: horizon,
        body, dot)
    } else {
      grid(columns: (auto, 1fr), column-gutter: 6pt, align: horizon,
        dot, body)
    }
  )
}

#set text(size: 10pt)

#grid(
  columns: (1fr, 2.2cm, 1fr),   // left column | drawing gutter | right column
  align: top,
  // --- left: Git commands (plus one GitHub-side action, item 11) ---
  stack(spacing: 7pt,
    item([1. `git clone <url>`]),
    item([2. `git add <file>` / stage]),
    item([3. `git commit -m "..."`]),
    item([4. `git push`]),
    item([5. `git pull`]),
    item([6. `git status`]),
    item([7. `git log`]),
    item([8. `git branch <name>` / checkout]),
    item([9. `git revert <commit>`]),
    item([10. `git add` + `git commit` (on GitHub)]),
    item([11. open a pull request #text(size: 8pt)[(GitHub action, not a Git command)]]),
  ),
  // --- middle: empty space to draw lines across ---
  [],
  // --- right: GUI actions, out of order ---
  stack(spacing: 7pt,
    item(side: left)[A. Click the *down-arrow* (pull) button in the RStudio Git pane],
    item(side: left)[B. Write a commit message, then click *Commit*],
    item(side: left)[C. On GitHub, click *Add file > Create new file* or *Upload files*, then *Commit changes*],
    item(side: left)[D. Copy the HTTPS URL, then *File > New Project > Version Control > Git* in RStudio],
    item(side: left)[E. Click the *up-arrow* (push) button in the RStudio Git pane],
    item(side: left)[F. Tick the *checkbox* next to a file in the RStudio Git pane],
    item(side: left)[G. On GitHub, open the *Pull requests* tab and click *New pull request*],
    item(side: left)[H. Read the file list and status flags shown in the RStudio Git pane],
    item(side: left)[I. Click the *branch dropdown* in the Git pane and choose *New Branch*],
    item(side: left)[J. Right-click a commit in *History* and choose *Revert*, or use the Git pane menu],
    item(side: left)[K. Click *History* (the clock icon) in the RStudio Git pane],
  ),
)

#pagebreak()
```

## Facilitator answer key

Do not print this page for learners.

| Command | Answer | GUI action |
| --- | :---: | --- |
| 1. `git clone <url>` | **D** | File > New Project > Version Control > Git |
| 2. `git add` / stage | **F** | Tick the checkbox next to a file in the Git pane |
| 3. `git commit -m "..."` | **B** | Write a message, click Commit |
| 4. `git push` | **E** | Up-arrow (push) button in the Git pane |
| 5. `git pull` | **A** | Down-arrow (pull) button in the Git pane |
| 6. `git status` | **H** | Read the file list and status flags in the Git pane |
| 7. `git log` | **K** | History (clock icon) in the Git pane |
| 8. `git branch` / checkout | **I** | Branch dropdown > New Branch |
| 9. `git revert <commit>` | **J** | Right-click a commit in History > Revert |
| 10. `git add` + `commit` on GitHub | **C** | Add file / Upload files, then Commit changes |
| 11. open a pull request *(GitHub action)* | **G** | Pull requests tab > New pull request |

### Talking points while reviewing

- Every button press ran a real command. Nothing was hidden or "easier"; it was
  the same operation.
- `git add` and `git commit` are two steps. In the RStudio pane you see this
  clearly: tick the box (add), then Commit. On GitHub the web editor bundles
  both into one "Commit changes" click.
- `git revert` makes a new commit that undoes an earlier one; it does not erase
  history. In the GUI this is a right-click on a commit in History. Good moment
  to contrast it with deleting or force-pushing.
- A pull request is not a Git command in the same sense; it is a GitHub feature
  built on top of branches you pushed. That is why it is called out separately
  on the sheet. Good moment to name where Git ends and GitHub begins.
- Tie-in to the agentic follow-up: an agent driving Git speaks these same
  command names. Knowing the mapping is what lets you read and check what the
  agent does.
